title: System Design Equation
caption: Senior frontend design combines architecture, UI, performance, scalability, and user experience into one operating model.

title: Component Thinking Versus System Thinking
caption: Component thinking optimizes the immediate screen. System thinking makes the surrounding contracts explicit before implementation.

title: Requirements-Before-Code Flow
caption: The senior path moves from constraints to scale pressure, bottlenecks, design options, and explicit trade-offs.

title: Real-Time Frontend Architecture
caption: A resilient real-time UI separates transport lifecycle, subscriptions, event processing, state, and rendering.

title: WebSocket Lifecycle State Machine
caption: Connection lifecycle should account for auth, subscriptions, degraded mode, reconnect, and terminal close.

title: Event Reconciliation Sequence
caption: Events should be validated, deduplicated, ordered, merged, and rendered through stable state boundaries.

title: High-Density Table Architecture
caption: Dense data views need a deliberate flow from query model to API delegation, cache shape, virtualized viewport, and row rendering.

title: Schema-Driven Rendering Pipeline
caption: A safe dynamic UI pipeline resolves schema against context, selects registered renderers, validates input, and submits through explicit contracts.

title: State Classification Matrix
caption: Classify state by owner, lifetime, persistence, shareability, and sync need before choosing storage or libraries.

title: Approval Workflow State Machine
caption: Workflow state defines allowed transitions and recovery paths for a multi-step approval flow.

title: Rendering Strategy Decision Matrix
caption: Rendering strategy should follow freshness, personalization, cacheability, interactivity, and first-content requirements.

title: Design System Operating Model
caption: A frontend platform connects design, tokens, components, documentation, governance, and adoption into one operating loop.

title: Frontend Failure Taxonomy
caption: Different failure classes need different containment, user messaging, retry behavior, and telemetry.

title: Error Boundary Placement Map
caption: Boundaries should be placed around app shell, routes, critical sections, and optional widgets so one failure does not erase the whole experience.

title: Retry and Fallback Sequence
caption: A resilient sequence classifies the error, chooses retry or fallback, preserves useful state, and records telemetry.

title: Frontend Observability Pipeline
caption: Browser events should flow through a telemetry SDK into collection, dashboards, alerts, and incident response.

title: Authentication and Session Flow
caption: A secure frontend auth flow separates browser navigation, identity provider authentication, backend session creation, and API authorization.

title: RBAC Rendering Pipeline
caption: User claims should be evaluated against policy to produce route, data, and action permissions before the UI renders affordances.

title: Browser Security Boundary Map
caption: The browser contains untrusted code execution, storage, network access, third-party scripts, extensions, and user-controlled inputs.

title: CSP and Third-Party Script Governance
description: CSP and third-party script governance architecture
caption: Third-party scripts should pass ownership, purpose, consent, loading, CSP, and monitoring checks before they execute.

title: Senior Frontend System Design Answer Flow
description: Senior frontend system design answer flow
caption: A strong answer moves from clarification to constraints, architecture, trade-offs, failure handling, and next steps.

title: Requirements-to-Architecture Map
caption: Requirements should map to concrete architecture decisions across data, rendering, state, UX, security, and operations.

title: Trade-Off Matrix for Implementation Options
caption: A senior answer compares options by user impact, operational complexity, reversibility, performance, and failure behavior.

title: Interview Evaluation Rubric
caption: Interviewers evaluate clarity, constraints, architecture, trade-offs, failure handling, communication, and leadership judgment.
